metadata.title: The Language Window: The Neuroscience Behind Early Music Exposure

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. A newborn can initially perceive the sound contrasts of every language on Earth. During the first twelve months the brain tunes itself toward the languages it hears. Music is an ancient pedagogical technology that enriches this sensitive auditory period. The infrastructure built in that window predicts language outcomes at 13, 16, and 24 months.

artifactLines: A newborn can initially perceive the sound contrasts of every language on Earth.
During the first twelve months the brain tunes itself toward the languages it hears.
Music is an ancient pedagogical technology that enriches this sensitive auditory period.
The infrastructure built in that window predicts language outcomes at 13, 16, and 24 months.

beats.narration_text: The idea that a newborn can hear every language on Earth and then narrows down is what I want to understand mechanically. Help me connect the sound environment to the later outcomes. Explain: (1) what physically changes in the auditory system during that first-year sensitive period as it tunes to native sounds, (2) why music counts as an ancient pedagogical technology that feeds this window, and (3) how researchers actually measure the language differences that show up at 13, 16, and 24 months.

beats.narration_text: Children raised in language-rich, music-rich, interaction-rich environments experience the sensitive period differently than children in impoverished auditory environments. The neural infrastructure built during those twelve months predicts language outcomes measurably at 13 months, 16 months, 24 months, and beyond.
